past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 6 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Mild depression, managed with sertraline for 3 years
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 68
Mother alive with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Brother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 56
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, 2-3 times per week
social_history.occupation: High school English teacher, currently on medical leave for the past 2 weeks
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and one teenage daughter in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 3 miles daily, stopped 6 weeks ago due to fatigue and headaches

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Appears mildly anxious. Speech is fluent but with occasional word-finding pauses — she substitutes circumlocutions for specific nouns (e.g., 'the thing you write with' for pen). Comprehension intact for simple commands but makes one error on a three-step command. Attention mildly impaired — makes two errors on serial sevens. Recall is 2 out of 3 words at 5 minutes. Fund of knowledge appropriate for education level.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation but patient reports subjective dimness in the right superior quadrant. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No facial asymmetry. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: 5/5. Left upper extremity: 5/5 grip, 4+/5 deltoid, 5/5 triceps and biceps, no pronator drift. Left lower extremity: 5/5 hip flexion and knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact bilaterally. Pinprick intact. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at ankles.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ right, 2+ left. Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No dysdiadochokinesia. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal stride length. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Mini-Mental State Examination score 26/30 (lost points on recall and serial sevens). Boston Naming Test: 13/15, with errors on low-frequency items.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive word-finding difficulty and personality change over 2 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Linda Kowalski is a 52-year-old right-handed woman and high school English teacher who presents with a 2-month history of progressive cognitive and behavioral changes, initially attributed to stress and worsening depression. Her husband first noticed something was wrong approximately 8 weeks ago when she began struggling to find words during conversations, occasionally substituting incorrect words or trailing off mid-sentence. As an English teacher, her vocabulary had always been exceptional, and these lapses were immediately noticeable to family and colleagues. Over the following weeks, she became increasingly irritable and short-tempered, which was uncharacteristic. She snapped at students in class on several occasions and had two arguments with colleagues over trivial matters, prompting the principal to suggest she take a leave of absence. Her husband also reports that she has become somewhat apathetic, losing interest in reading novels — previously her greatest passion — and spending hours staring at the television without engaging with the content. Approximately 6 weeks ago, she began complaining of dull, pressure-like headaches, predominantly on the left side, that were worse in the morning and would gradually improve over the course of the day. The headaches were initially responsive to ibuprofen but have become more persistent over the past 3 weeks. Three weeks ago, she had an episode where her right hand began shaking involuntarily for approximately 30 seconds while she was eating breakfast. She did not lose consciousness but felt confused for several minutes afterward. Her husband witnessed the episode and described rhythmic jerking of the right hand that she could not suppress. She was evaluated by her primary care physician, who increased her sertraline dose, suspecting worsening depression. However, her symptoms continued to progress, and she was referred to neurology. At the outpatient neurology visit one week ago, the examining neurologist was concerned by her word-finding difficulty and subtle left-sided motor findings, and ordered an urgent MRI of the brain. The MRI revealed a left temporal lobe lesion, and she was admitted for further workup. The patient herself acknowledges feeling 'not right' but minimizes the extent of her symptoms. She attributes her personality changes to stress at work and denies feeling depressed, though she admits she has been sleeping more than usual and has lost approximately 4 kg over the past two months without trying.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha, reactive, slightly lower amplitude on the left
background.symmetry: Mild amplitude asymmetry with lower voltages over the left temporal region

findings.clinical_correlation: Suggests underlying structural lesion in the left temporal region
Regional cerebral dysfunction, may indicate peri-lesional cortical irritability

artifacts.effect: Intermittent obscuration of temporal activity, partially mitigated with filter adjustments

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Accentuated left temporal slowing during and for 2 minutes following hyperventilation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response bilaterally, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to continuous left temporal polymorphic delta slowing and intermittent left frontotemporal rhythmic delta activity. No definite epileptiform discharges were identified, though the regional slowing suggests underlying structural pathology and peri-lesional cortical dysfunction. Clinical correlation with neuroimaging is recommended.
eeg.limitations: Intermittent muscle artifact partially obscured temporal activity in some epochs.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged EEG monitoring if clinical seizures are suspected
Repeat EEG after treatment if clinical change occurs

findings.location: Left temporal lobe, centered in the superior and middle temporal gyri, extending to the temporal operculum and insular cortex

signal_characteristics.T1_pre_contrast: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense with small foci of T1 hyperintensity suggesting hemorrhage or proteinaceous content
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Irregular, heterogeneous enhancement without classic complete ring pattern; enhancement is thick and nodular along the medial and posterior margins
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense with surrounding FLAIR signal extending into the left temporal white matter, external capsule, and posteriorly toward the angular gyrus
signal_characteristics.DWI: Mildly restricted diffusion along the enhancing margins, ADC values reduced but not as markedly as in abscess
signal_characteristics.SWI: Foci of susceptibility artifact within the mass suggesting microhemorrhage or calcification

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the left temporal horn of the lateral ventricle. No significant midline shift (2 mm leftward falx deviation).
findings.borders: Ill-defined, infiltrative margins, particularly along the medial temporal extent and insular involvement

findings.location: Left temporal and parietal white matter

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense signal extending beyond the enhancing mass into adjacent white matter tracts

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to mild sulcal effacement over the left temporal convexity

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions elsewhere in the brain parenchyma
Major intracranial vessels patent on MRA with no evidence of encasement or occlusion
Left temporal horn mildly compressed but not trapped
mri.impression: Irregular heterogeneously enhancing left temporal mass with infiltrative margins, surrounding vasogenic edema, and mild mass effect. The lesion is centered in the superior and middle temporal gyri with extension to the insular cortex. The enhancement pattern is thick and nodular rather than a classic ring, and the infiltrative margins raise concern for high-grade glioma. The absence of classic central necrosis and ring enhancement makes this somewhat atypical for glioblastoma, though the heterogeneous enhancement, restricted diffusion at margins, and infiltrative behavior favor a high-grade astrocytic neoplasm. Differential considerations include anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO grade 3) versus glioblastoma (WHO grade 4).

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Irregular heterogeneous enhancement, infiltrative margins, peri-tumoral edema
Solitary lesion argues against, but cannot exclude without systemic workup; lacks the spherical well-demarcated appearance typical of metastasis
Heterogeneous enhancement is somewhat atypical for lymphoma which tends to be homogeneous; no immunocompromise history
No open-ring enhancement pattern, no history of multiple sclerosis, mass effect relatively proportional to lesion size

recommended_actions: MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite ratios
MR perfusion to assess relative cerebral blood volume
Consider stereotactic biopsy for definitive histological and molecular diagnosis
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to exclude primary malignancy if metastasis remains on differential

Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; nonspecific, may be seen in malignancy

labs.interpretation: Laboratory values are largely within normal limits, providing adequate baseline for potential surgical intervention and chemotherapy. Mild elevation of LDH is nonspecific but may be associated with malignancy. ESR mildly elevated. CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and hepatic panel are all normal, confirming adequate organ function for treatment planning.

findings.location: Left temporal mass, corresponding to the enhancing component

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV ratio 2.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter)
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Elevated regional cerebral blood flow in the enhancing portions
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated permeability metrics consistent with disrupted blood-brain barrier

findings.location: Non-enhancing FLAIR hyperintense region surrounding the mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Mildly elevated rCBV (ratio 1.4) in a small focus within the non-enhancing T2/FLAIR abnormality anteromedially, suggesting possible infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Slightly increased relative to contralateral white matter

additional_observations: Elevated rCBV in the enhancing component is consistent with neovascularity and supports high-grade glioma
The focus of elevated perfusion beyond the enhancing margin suggests infiltrative tumor extending into the peri-lesional edema zone
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates elevated rCBV within the enhancing component of the left temporal mass (ratio 2.8), supporting the diagnosis of a high-grade glial neoplasm. A small focus of elevated perfusion in the non-enhancing peritumoral region suggests tumor infiltration beyond the visible enhancing margin, which has implications for surgical and radiation planning.
recommended_actions: Incorporate perfusion data into surgical planning
Consider extended resection margin if safely feasible given proximity to eloquent cortex

findings.location: Left temporal mass, single-voxel spectroscopy placed within the enhancing component

signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present, indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

findings.location: Non-enhancing FLAIR abnormality adjacent to the mass (anterior voxel)

signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: No significant lipid or lactate peak

additional_observations: The spectroscopy profile of the enhancing mass is characteristic of high-grade glioma with elevated choline (high cellularity/membrane turnover), reduced NAA (neuronal loss), and lipid/lactate peaks (necrosis/hypoxia)

Peritumoral spectroscopy abnormalities with mild choline elevation suggest infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin, distinguishing this from metastatic disease where peritumoral edema is typically pure vasogenic
output.impression: MR spectroscopy findings are consistent with a high-grade glial neoplasm. Elevated choline, reduced NAA, and prominent lipid/lactate peaks within the enhancing mass are characteristic. Peritumoral choline elevation supports infiltrative glioma rather than metastatic disease.
recommended_actions: Proceed with stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection for tissue diagnosis
Obtain molecular profiling including IDH mutation, MGMT methylation, and 1p/19q codeletion status

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Normal size and enhancement bilaterally, no masses
No pelvic masses or lymphadenopathy. Uterus and ovaries unremarkable.

output.interpretation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast is unremarkable. No evidence of primary malignancy or metastatic disease elsewhere in the body. This finding argues against the brain lesion being a metastasis and supports a primary brain tumor as the most likely diagnosis.

Histopathology.value: Markedly increased with nuclear atypia and pleomorphism

Histopathology.clinical_significance: High proliferative activity consistent with high-grade neoplasm

Molecular markers.value: Wildtype (negative for R132H and other IDH mutations by sequencing)
Molecular markers.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype glioblastoma carries worse prognosis than IDH-mutant tumors
Unmethylated MGMT is associated with reduced response to temozolomide chemotherapy
Absence of 1p/19q codeletion rules out oligodendroglioma
TERT promoter mutation in the context of IDH-wildtype supports glioblastoma diagnosis
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; may be relevant for targeted therapy trials
High proliferative index consistent with aggressive high-grade tumor

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy reveals glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4. Histology demonstrates markedly increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, elevated mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and focal palisading necrosis — classic features of glioblastoma. Molecular profiling shows IDH-wildtype status, unmethylated MGMT promoter, TERT promoter mutation, and EGFR amplification. The absence of 1p/19q codeletion excludes oligodendroglioma. The combination of IDH-wildtype status and unmethylated MGMT promoter indicates a poor prognostic profile with expected reduced chemosensitivity to temozolomide.
abnormal_values_summary: MGMT promoter unmethylated (reduced temozolomide response expected)

findings.location: Left inferior frontal gyrus (pars opercularis and triangularis)

signal_characteristics.activation: Robust BOLD activation during verb generation and sentence completion tasks

findings.location: Left posterior superior temporal gyrus and supramarginal gyrus

signal_characteristics.activation: BOLD activation present but reduced in amplitude compared to normative data; activation cluster is displaced posterosuperiorly by the mass
signal_characteristics.laterality_index: 0.58 (left dominant for receptive language, mildly reduced suggesting possible reorganization)

signal_characteristics.activation: Normal BOLD activation during right hand finger tapping and tongue movement tasks
signal_characteristics.distance_to_tumor: Approximately 2.8 cm from the superior margin of the enhancing mass to the nearest motor cortex activation

signal_characteristics.relationship: The posterior-superior margin of the tumor lies within 8 mm of displaced Wernicke area activation. The arcuate fasciculus on DTI tractography appears deviated but not disrupted.

additional_observations: Patient is left-hemisphere dominant for language
Wernicke area activation is partially displaced by the mass but remains in close proximity to the tumor margin
DTI tractography shows the arcuate fasciculus deviated superiorly by the mass but maintaining structural continuity
Motor cortex is sufficiently distant from the tumor to allow safe surgical approach from that direction
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates left hemisphere language dominance. Broca area activation is robust and located well away from the tumor. Wernicke area activation is partially displaced by the mass and lies within 8 mm of the posterior-superior tumor margin, placing receptive language function at risk during surgical resection. DTI shows deviation but not disruption of the arcuate fasciculus. These findings suggest that maximal safe resection is feasible but will require intraoperative language mapping (awake craniotomy) to preserve Wernicke area function.
recommended_actions: Intraoperative cortical and subcortical language mapping (awake craniotomy) is recommended
Neurosurgical planning should incorporate fMRI and DTI data to define safe resection boundaries
Pre-operative speech and language baseline assessment recommended

output.query: glioblastoma IDH-wildtype MGMT unmethylated standard of care treatment 2024

results.title: Stupp Protocol: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.relevance: Landmark trial establishing standard of care: maximal safe resection followed by concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy in 30 fractions with daily temozolomide 75 mg/m2) followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 of 28-day cycle)

results.title: MGMT promoter methylation status and temozolomide benefit in glioblastoma
results.relevance: MGMT unmethylated patients derive less benefit from temozolomide but still receive it as standard of care; median survival approximately 12-14 months vs 21 months for methylated

results.title: Tumor treating fields (TTFields) for newly diagnosed glioblastoma
results.relevance: TTFields added to maintenance temozolomide improved median overall survival to 20.9 months vs 16 months; FDA-approved addition to standard of care

output.summary: Standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype remains the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy with temozolomide) and adjuvant temozolomide. For MGMT unmethylated tumors, the benefit of temozolomide is reduced but it remains part of standard therapy. Tumor treating fields (TTFields/Optune) are an FDA-approved addition to maintenance therapy. Clinical trial enrollment should be considered, particularly for MGMT unmethylated tumors where alternative approaches may be beneficial. Bevacizumab may be considered at recurrence. Dexamethasone is used for peritumoral edema management, and levetiracetam is standard for seizure prophylaxis in the perioperative period.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during radiation, then 150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 of 28-day cycle
interactions: Sertraline: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction, but monitor for additive nausea
Levothyroxine: No direct interaction, but monitor thyroid function as temozolomide may affect absorption timing
contraindications: Severe hepatic or renal impairment (patient has normal function — not applicable)
Known hypersensitivity to dacarbazine (same drug class)
warnings: Myelosuppression: Monitor CBC weekly during concurrent chemoradiation, and on days 22 and 29 of each adjuvant cycle

Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) required during concurrent phase — NOTE: patient has penicillin allergy but not sulfa allergy, so TMP-SMX can be used; if sulfa allergy were present, alternative prophylaxis with pentamidine or dapsone would be needed
Hepatotoxicity: Monitor liver function tests periodically
Teratogenicity: Verify negative pregnancy test and counsel on contraception (patient is 52, likely perimenopausal)
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) — may be considered for MGMT unmethylated tumors based on CeTeG trial data
Bevacizumab — no OS benefit in newly diagnosed GBM but may be considered at recurrence
Clinical trial enrollment recommended for MGMT unmethylated tumors

differential.key_features: Irregular enhancement without classic necrosis could suggest grade 3 rather than grade 4; requires histological confirmation to distinguish

Solitary enhancing lesion could represent metastasis; however, infiltrative margins, lack of well-defined spherical shape, and peritumoral spectroscopic abnormalities argue against; negative systemic imaging workup further reduces likelihood
Can present as enhancing mass in immunocompetent patients; typically homogeneously enhancing and periventricular; heterogeneous enhancement and temporal location are atypical for lymphoma
Can mimic tumor with ring enhancement; typically shows open-ring enhancement, less mass effect for size, and occurs in younger patients with MS history; no prior demyelinating events in this patient

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history and perform comprehensive neurological examination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive word-finding difficulty, personality change, headaches worse in morning, and a witnessed focal motor seizure; exam shows anomia, subtle left-sided weakness, and mild cognitive impairment

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Irregular heterogeneously enhancing left temporal mass with surrounding edema and mild mass effect

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, hepatic panel, and LDH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal labs with mildly elevated LDH; establishes baseline for surgical and chemotherapeutic planning

optimal_actions.action: Order EEG given history of witnessed seizure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal focal slowing consistent with structural lesion; guides antiepileptic drug management

optimal_actions.action: Start antiepileptic medication (levetiracetam) given witnessed seizure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure control; levetiracetam preferred due to lack of hepatic enzyme induction that could interact with chemotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Start dexamethasone for peritumoral edema and headache management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in headaches and potentially mild improvement in focal neurological symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Order MR perfusion and MR spectroscopy for advanced tumor characterization
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV and elevated choline/reduced NAA confirming high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out systemic primary malignancy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative systemic workup, supporting primary brain tumor rather than metastasis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain neurosurgical consultation for biopsy or resection planning
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning initiated; given proximity to eloquent cortex, awake craniotomy with intraoperative mapping is recommended

optimal_actions.action: Order functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Wernicke area displaced but in close proximity to tumor, requiring intraoperative mapping; motor cortex sufficiently distant

optimal_actions.action: Perform maximal safe resection or stereotactic biopsy with molecular testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, MGMT unmethylated on tissue analysis

optimal_actions.action: Initiate Stupp protocol: concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy + temozolomide) followed by adjuvant temozolomide
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard of care treatment initiated; PCP prophylaxis prescribed during concurrent phase

optimal_actions.action: Discuss prognosis and goals of care with patient and family; refer to neuro-oncology and palliative care
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Informed consent and shared decision-making regarding treatment plan

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the lesion
Start antiepileptic medication given witnessed seizure
Rule out metastatic disease with systemic imaging
Obtain tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)
Neurosurgical consultation for resection or biopsy
Pre-surgical functional MRI given tumor proximity to eloquent cortex
Initiate Stupp protocol after tissue diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture without neuroimaging — risk of herniation with mass lesion and mass effect
Starting enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, carbamazepine) — interfere with chemotherapy metabolism
Withholding corticosteroids in the presence of significant peritumoral edema and symptoms
Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue diagnosis
Delaying neurosurgical consultation in favor of prolonged observation
Prescribing anticoagulation without careful risk-benefit analysis given tumor hemorrhage risk
key_reasoning_points: Progressive word-finding difficulty in an English teacher represents a significant functional decline that should raise concern for a structural left hemispheric lesion rather than depression alone
The combination of progressive cognitive symptoms, personality change, morning headaches, and a witnessed focal motor seizure strongly suggests an intracranial mass lesion

The MRI shows atypical features for classic glioblastoma (no complete ring enhancement, no frank central necrosis), making the distinction between high-grade and lower-grade glioma challenging without tissue diagnosis
Advanced MRI techniques (perfusion and spectroscopy) provide additional non-invasive support for high-grade neoplasm but cannot replace histological and molecular confirmation
Tumor location in the dominant temporal lobe near Wernicke area necessitates functional MRI and potentially awake craniotomy to preserve language function
MGMT promoter methylation status has important prognostic and potentially therapeutic implications — unmethylated status indicates reduced benefit from temozolomide
Levetiracetam is preferred over enzyme-inducing AEDs in brain tumor patients because it does not interfere with chemotherapy metabolism
Negative systemic imaging workup supports primary brain tumor over metastasis, which changes the therapeutic approach entirely
